result:
I find myself thinking about rivers today. Not the grand, named ones—the Niles and Amazons and Mississippis that shape continents and history books—but the small, anonymous ones. The kind you find at the end of a dirt road, where the only sounds are the water's whispered conversation with the stones and the distant complaint of a crow.

There’s a particular magic to a forgotten stream. It has no delusions of grandeur. It doesn’t rush toward the ocean with the impatient fury of a river that knows its own importance. It meanders. It gets distracted by a fallen log, creating a tiny, bubbling rapid for beetles to navigate. It pauses in deep, clear pools where light filters through willow leaves and dapples the sandy bottom. It is a world in miniature, entire sagas playing out in its eddies: the determined journey of a leaf-boat, the skittering dance of water striders on a surface tension they understand better than I ever will.

It makes me think of time, and how we perceive it. We live in the Amazon River of time—a relentless, forward-charging current, marked by deadlines and milestones, flowing toward some vast, unknowable sea. We measure our progress by the banks flying past. But what of the creek-time? The time that loops back on itself in a still pool, that slows to admire the moss on a single stone, that holds the memory of last night’s rain and last year’s drought in its same gentle flow? That time exists, too. It’s the time of a deep breath, of watching a pot of water *almost* boil, of a shared silence that says more than words.

We’re taught to build dams across our creek-time. To channel all our resources into the powerful, productive flow. We harness it for energy, for output. But I wonder what silts up behind those dams. The quiet observations. The pointless, beautiful daydreams. The patience to watch a spider rebuild her web. The peace of existing without a measurable purpose.

Maybe that’s why I’m drawn to these small waters. They are a kind of resistance. In their obscurity, they are free. They follow gravity’s gentle suggestion, not a mapmaker’s decree. They change course when the land does, embracing adaptability over stubborn destiny. They are humble, and in their humility, they are utterly complete.

I think I’ll go find one this afternoon. Not to fish, or to hike along it, or to photograph it for proof. Just to sit on its bank, let the clock in my mind melt away like sugar in creek-time, and remember how to meander.

There’s a whole philosophy in the unhurried journey of a stream that no one but the heron and the dragonfly will ever see. It murmurs a quiet, persistent truth: that not everything worthy needs to be wide, deep, or on the map. Some things just need to flow, and in flowing, sustain their own small, perfect universe.
